title: Sum of Beauty of All Substrings

content.problemStatement:
You are given a string **SSS** consisting of lowercase English letters.

The **beauty** of a substring is defined as the difference between the frequency of the **most frequent character** and the frequency of the **least frequent character** in that substring, considering **only characters that appear at least once**.

Your task is to calculate the **sum of beauty** of all possible substrings of the given string **SSS**.

Function Declaration

**Function Name:**



**beautySumbeautySumbeautySum**



**Parameters:**



**sss** (`string`)
A string consisting of lowercase English letters.



**Return Value:**



Returns an `int` representing the **sum of beauty of all substrings** of the string.



Input Format
The first line contains an integer **TTT** — the number of test cases.


Each test case contains a single line with the string **SSS**.




Output Format
For each test case, print a single integer — the **sum of beauty of all substrings** of **SSS**.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T — the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a non‑empty string S consisting of lowercase English letters.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer on its own line — the sum of beauty of all substrings of S.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For "abcab" only substrings "bcab", "abca" and "abcab" have non‑zero beauty, each contributing 1, so the sum is 3. For "zzxyzz" the total beauty over all substrings equals 12.
Substrings: a(0), ab(0), aba(1), b(0), ba(0), a(0). Only "aba" has beauty 1, so the answer is 1.
A single character substring has max and min frequency both equal to 1, thus beauty 0.

solution.approach: For each starting position i we extend the substring to the right one character at a time. While extending we maintain a frequency array of size 26. After adding a new character we scan the 26 counters to obtain the current maximum and minimum frequencies among characters that appear at least once. Their difference is the beauty of the current substring and is added to the answer. The outer loop runs O(N) times, the inner loop O(N) times and the scan of the 26 counters is O(26)=O(1). Hence the total complexity per test case is O(N^2) which is easily fast enough for N ≤ 500.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N^2) per test case (N = length of the string).
